summary: Jeff Atwood argues that most passwords are dangerously short, demonstrating through concrete math that even random 8-character passwords can be cracked in seconds by modern GPU arrays. He shows that password length matters more than complexity, with each additional character exponentially increasing crack time. The post advocates for a minimum of 12 characters for any password and encourages developers to either eliminate passwords entirely through OAuth, use browser-based password managers, or implement slow hashing algorithms like scrypt. Atwood grounds his argument in the reality that database breaches are inevitable, making offline hash cracking the primary threat model.
key_points: Users will always choose simple passwords and reuse them across services - this is an unchangeable human behavior developers must design around
A random 8-character password can be cracked in 2.2 seconds by a GPU array, while 12 characters pushes it to 1+ years
The best password is one you don't have to store - OAuth and browser password managers are preferable to custom password systems
Developers must assume passwords will be hashed with the weakest possible algorithm and plan accordingly
GPU-resistant hashing algorithms like scrypt with appropriate work factors are essential, not optional
money_quotes: The best password is one you don't have to store.
Unless your password is at least 12 characters, you are vulnerable.

Building an array of, say, 24 consumer grade GPUs that are optimized for speed hashing, is well within the reach of the average law enforcement agency and pretty much any small business that can afford a $40k equipment charge.
No matter what you tell them, users will always choose simple passwords.
This kind of leak happens all the time. And it will continue to happen forever.
You have to assume that every password you use will be hashed with the lamest, fastest hash out there.
Go lay down and put a warm towel over your face.
key_insight: Password security isn't about complexity rules or user education - it's about length, and 12 characters is the new minimum for surviving modern GPU-powered hash cracking.
